ATLO 101  -  Foundation Module
LESSON  F.6
Lesson F.6 — Interactive

Thermal Control

Space is an extreme thermal environment -- from over 120C in direct sunlight to below -100C in shadow. The thermal subsystem keeps every component within its operating range.

Real hardware — explore the photo

Tap the numbered markers to identify what you are looking at.
REAL HARDWARE

MLI blanket installation on the Landsat TIRS instrument

A technician at NASA Goddard fits a gold multi-layer insulation blanket to the TIRS instrument. Tap the numbered markers to explore what MLI installation really involves.

NASA/GSFC/Rebecca Roth

Thermal critical components

Click each component to see what it does and what ATLO verifies.

What Thermal Control Does

The thermal control subsystem manages heat using both passive and active techniques to maintain thermal balance through sunlight, shadow, and varying power states. There is no convection in vacuum -- heat is rejected only through radiation.

What ATLO Engineers Verify

TVAC -- Thermal Vacuum -- testing is the most comprehensive thermal verification. The spacecraft is placed in a vacuum chamber and subjected to extreme hot and cold temperatures. ATLO engineers monitor every subsystem throughout, running functional tests at temperature plateaus.

KEY POINT

MLI installation is critical during mechanical integration. A poorly installed MLI blanket -- compressed, torn, or improperly grounded -- can create a thermal anomaly on orbit that is impossible to fix.
